topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I was reaching for my late-night study notes, and my hand brushed against something tucked under the loose floorboard near my desk. It was my journal. Not just lying there, but open. The ink was familiar, the looping handwriting undeniably mine, detailing anxieties about exams and vague, hopeful dreams for the future. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air. When I finally looked up, Liam was sitting at his own desk, ostensibly engrossed in a textbook, but his gaze flickered towards me, a subtle, almost imperceptible shift in his expression that sent a cold tremor through my chest. The silence in the room stretched taut, thick with unspoken accusation. I felt my cheeks flush a deep, painful red, the urge to simply vanish overwhelming.

[story 2]

It started as a small prickle of unease. I noticed my diary, usually kept under my pillow, was sitting on the corner of Liam’s desk, slightly ajar. Curiosity, that treacherous beast, compelled me to look. The pages were filled with my most vulnerable thoughts—the fears I hadn't shared with anyone else. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. When I finally found myself confronting him, the air crackled. He looked up, his eyes wide, a flicker of something unreadable passing over them before he quickly looked away, fiddling with a pen. I mumbled something incoherent, my voice sounding thin and reedy, and retreated to the bathroom, leaning against the cool tile, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my heart.

[story 3]

The realization dawned slowly, a creeping dread that settled deep in my bones. I found the journal tucked inside my backpack, spine cracked, its pages scattered in a way that suggested it had been handled, not just placed. Every entry was there, every secret confession laid bare. Seeing it, knowing he had been reading them, made the world tilt. I stood there, frozen by the doorway, my hands trembling so violently I had to press them against my thighs to stop the shaking. When Liam finally spoke, his tone was low, measured, lacking any real defense. He didn't look at the book; he looked directly at me, and in that steady gaze, I felt an overwhelming sense of inadequacy wash over me, a heavy weight pressing down on my shoulders.
